title: OUT_FULL_16 and OUT_NO_ORIENT share the same bit value (8), making them mutually ambiguous
description: Two distinct constants are both defined as `8`: `OUT_FULL_16 = 8` (line 551) controls emission of a full-resolution RGB16 buffer, while `OUT_NO_ORIENT = 8` (line 556) suppresses apply_orientation. Any caller that passes flag `8` intending to request the full-16 buffer simultaneously disables orientation, and vice versa. The process_orf_impl code at line 743 uses `OUT_FULL_16` to gate the packed buffer and at line 800 uses `OUT_NO_ORIENT` to skip orientation — both test `output_flags & 8`. The public doc comment for `process_dng_with_flags` (line 2207) documents bit 8 as 'full RGB16 (M3)' with no mention of NO_ORIENT, so callers cannot know they are activating both behaviours simultaneously.

title: DNG/CR2 ProcessResult uses denominator 1 for absent rational EXIF fields; ORF uses denominator 0 as the absence sentinel
description: The ORF path (process_orf_impl, line 843) sets absent rationals as `(0, 0)` with the comment 'JS checks den==0 before dividing'. The DNG/CR2 path (process_dng_impl, line 1929-1936) sets them to `(0, 1)` — a non-zero denominator. JS consumers that gate on `den == 0` to detect absence will compute 0/1 = 0 for DNG/CR2 files instead of treating the field as absent, leading to silently wrong EXIF display (e.g. showing focal length = 0mm, f/0 rather than 'unknown').
code_excerpt:
// ORF (line 843-848):
exposure_num: info.exposure.map(|(n, _)| n).unwrap_or(0),
exposure_den: info.exposure.map(|(_, d)| d).unwrap_or(0),  // 0 = absent
...
// DNG (line 1929-1935):
exposure_num: 0,
exposure_den: 1,  // NOT 0; JS sentinel broken
fnumber_num: 0,
fnumber_den: 1,

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: PerceptualComparer::new does not validate that ref_rgba length equals width*height*4
description: The constructor accepts `ref_rgba: &[u8]`, `width: usize`, and `height: usize` but performs no length check before passing them to `PerceptualCore::new`. If the JS caller passes mismatched dimensions (e.g. an empty slice with non-zero dims), the inner kernel will read out-of-bounds or produce silently wrong metrics. The scratch buffer is allocated from `width * height` without overflow check, so very large dims could also cause an OOM panic in WASM. By contrast, every other public WASM entry point in this file validates length and guards against overflow.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: PerceptualComparer::all_at does not validate that len <= scratch buffer length, risking out-of-bounds slice
description: The method slices `&buf[..len]` where `len` is caller-supplied and `buf` is the scratch buffer. If `input_ptr` was never called, the scratch buffer has the size allocated at construction (`n * 4`). A JS caller may pass `len` larger than that (e.g. a frame with more pixels than the reference), causing a panic in the slice operation. The method has no guard and no documented contract on the maximum valid `len`.

title: frame_stats uses the 4-lane word-hash (not FNV) for full-buffer case but the public doc promises frameHashInt is 'the exact FNV-1a value'
description: The docstring at line 2806 says 'frameHashInt is the exact FNV-1a value — bit-identical to the shipped JS hash'. However, the full-buffer path (line 2823) calls `fs_core_simd`, which uses the 4-lane word-hash whose hash identity explicitly differs from byte-FNV. Only the truncated path calls `fs_core_trunc_word`, also not exact FNV. The production exact-FNV SIMD kernel `fs_core_simd_exact` exists but is NOT used in `frame_stats`. Consumers that log `frameHashInt` and compare across native/WASM or across the transition will observe mismatches despite the promise of bit-identity.

title: take_rgb and rgb() are both public on ProcessResult but take_rgb empties the buffer; a caller invoking rgb() after take_rgb gets silently empty data
description: Both `take_rgb()` and `rgb()` are public wasm-bindgen methods that return the internal RGB8 buffer. `take_rgb` moves it out (returning empty on subsequent calls), while `rgb` always clones it. There is no documentation on either method warning that `take_rgb` invalidates subsequent `rgb()` or `take_rgba()` calls. JS callers that call `rgb()` first and `take_rgb()` second get inconsistent results silently, rather than an error. The `take_rgba` method (line 296) calls `rgb_to_rgba(&std::mem::take(&mut self.rgb))` and would similarly return an empty RGBA if called after `take_rgb`.

title: LookRenderer::new always delegates to new_with_options with apply_rotation=true but the wasm-bindgen constructor signature does not expose apply_rotation, leaving no way to construct the rotation-skipping variant from JS via new()
description: The `new` constructor is the wasm-bindgen `#[wasm_bindgen(constructor)]` and only calls `new_with_options(..., true)`. The `new_with_options` variant that takes `apply_rotation: bool` is exported but NOT marked `#[wasm_bindgen(constructor)]`, so it is callable from JS as a regular method — but JS `new LookRenderer(...)` always applies rotation. This is a capability exposure gap: the rotation-skipping fast path (documented as saving a full-buffer transpose per slider tick) cannot be accessed through the idiomatic JS constructor, making the optimisation invisible to consumers who discover the API via `new`.

title: rows_to_json hand-rolls JSON serialisation with no escaping of the filename field, allowing malformed JSON if a filename contains backslashes or quotes
description: The `file` field is written as `row.file.replace('\\', "/")` (line 206), which converts backslashes to forward slashes, but does not escape double-quotes, newlines, tabs, or other JSON-special characters. On Windows file systems, filenames can legally contain characters that would break JSON structure (e.g. a filename with `"` would terminate the JSON string prematurely). The resulting `benchmark/results_native.json` would be unparseable by downstream consumers that import it for comparison.

title: bench_jxl_roundtrip assigns decode_region_downsample_ms = decode_ms (a JXL decode timing) to all three format paths, making it semantically wrong for the 'decode work for the requested region' field
description: The comment on `decode_region_downsample_ms` in BenchRow (line 177) states it is 'the decode work performed for the requested region'. The DNG, CR2 and ORF paths all set this to `decode_ms` — i.e. the JXL re-decode time of the full image, not a region sub-decode. This conflates full-decode cost with ROI decode cost in the handoff summary table printed at the end, breaking the 'apples-to-apples with WASM onMetric' contract stated in the doc comment at line 299.

category: no input validation at public boundary
title: fstats_prepare accepts arbitrary w and h without overflow or size guard, enabling OOM in WASM
description: The `fstats_prepare` function allocates `w * h * 4` bytes without checking for overflow or a maximum size. On a 32-bit WASM heap, passing `w=65536, h=65536` would overflow `usize` and produce a zero-length or tiny allocation, after which the LCG fill loop would write out-of-bounds. Even without overflow, a pathologically large value can OOM the tab. Every other public WASM function with size arguments uses `checked_mul` and an explicit pixel cap.

category: no API schema / no contract testing
title: No versioning field or capability token on ProcessResult, making breaking additions invisible to callers who check only field presence
description: ProcessResult has grown 25+ fields over time (rgb, rgb16_lb, rgb16_thumb, rgb16_full, GPS, EXIF, look, perceptual, etc.). There is no `api_version` or `capabilities` field to allow JS callers to guard against forward-incompatible changes. As new optional outputs are added (e.g. OUT_FULL_16), callers consuming the struct from an older WASM bundle silently get zero-dim empty buffers with no way to distinguish 'feature not supported' from 'feature returned empty due to absent flag'. A version or feature-set bitmask in the struct would let JS code adapt gracefully.
